explicit_text: I moved to Estes Park when I was 14 years old, after my family relocated from Gold Hill. I came here on horseback, riding one of our horses for 24 hours along Highway 7. Estes Park felt like a carnival town to me back then, with lots of neon lights and arcades downtown. I went to Estes Park High School and eventually made my life and career here, including serving as fire chief for the Estes Park Fire Department.
implicit_text: I moved to a mountain town in Colorado when I was 14 years old, after my family relocated from a nearby mining community. I came here on horseback, riding one of our horses for 24 hours along a scenic state highway. That mountain town felt like a carnival town to me back then, with lots of neon lights and arcades downtown. I went to the local high school and eventually made my life and career here, including serving as fire chief for the local fire department.

explicit_text: Gold Hill was where my family lived before moving to Estes Park. It was a small mining town just west of Boulder, and with ten siblings, we made up half the population there. I remember riding a horse from Gold Hill to Estes Park because we didn't have a trailer, and that journey took me 24 hours. Leaving Gold Hill was a big change, especially moving to a larger community like Estes Park.

I didn't go into downtown that first time but had been there a few times earlier that summer with my family to see where we were moving.

explicit_text: During my high school years in Estes Park, I worked as a bellhop at the Stanley Hotel for a while. It was one of several jobs I took on to help earn extra money for my family. That experience was part of my early work life before I pursued other careers and eventually firefighting.

explicit_text: I worked at McGraw Ranch for two summers during my high school years. Like my job at the Stanley Hotel, it was a way to earn extra money for my family. These jobs gave me a variety of experiences growing up in Estes Park before I moved on to other careers.

explicit_text: In my role as fire chief, we operate under the Incident Command System. The first firefighter on scene acts as the incident commander and manages the fire unless it’s too large, then command is transferred up to officers or the chief. Often, I’m one of the first on scene, organizing resources, prioritizing rescue or suppression efforts, and sometimes working directly as a firefighter.
implicit_text: In my role as fire chief, we operate under a standardized emergency management system. The first firefighter on scene acts as the incident commander and manages the fire unless it’s too large, then command is transferred up to officers or the chief. Often, I’m one of the first on scene, organizing resources, prioritizing rescue or suppression efforts, and sometimes working directly as a firefighter.

explicit_text: I have been with the Estes Park Fire Department for 22 years, starting as a firefighter and eventually becoming fire chief. Over the years, I’ve held many roles including training officer, safety officer, assistant chief, and dive team member. I helped start the dive rescue team and EMS program, and I’ve seen the department grow from about 85 calls a year to 500 calls annually.

explicit_text: I originally come from upstate New York, not New York City, and left there in fifth grade. While I don’t have direct experiences with New York City, I sometimes compare the volunteer firefighting environment here in Estes Park to a city like New York, where the daily excitement and call volume are much higher.

explicit_text: I’ve lived most of my life in the United States, moving from upstate New York to Colorado. My experiences growing up and working in different parts of the country have shaped my career and personal life. Serving as a volunteer firefighter and fire chief here in Estes Park is part of my contribution to my community and country.
